# Loading a GIF in Svelte component (2025-01-19 22:43Z)

_**User (2025-01-19 22:43Z)**_

load a gif

---

_**Assistant**_

To load a GIF in your Svelte component, you need to set the `src` attribute of the `<img>` tag to the URL of the GIF. Here's how you can do it:

```svelte
<img src="path/to/your/image.gif" alt="Description of the GIF">
```

Replace `"path/to/your/image.gif"` with the actual path to your GIF file. The `alt` attribute is optional but recommended for accessibility.

---

_**User (2025-01-19 22:52Z)**_

background image source

---

_**Assistant**_

To set a background image in your Svelte component, you should use the `url()` function in your CSS. Here's how you can update your code:

```svelte
<div>
	<img src="SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg" alt="logo" />
</div>

<style>
	div {
		background-image: url('Softground-Hero.gif');
		background-size: cover; /* Optional: Adjusts the size of the background image */
		background-position: center; /* Optional: Centers the background image */
	}
</style>
```

This will set `Softground-Hero.gif` as the background image for the `<div>`. Adjust the `background-size` and `background-position` properties as needed.

_**User (2025-01-19 22:53Z)**_

animate logo

---

_**Assistant**_

To animate the logo in your Svelte component, you can use CSS animations. Here's an example of how to add a simple animation to the logo:

```svelte
<div>
	<img src="SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg" alt="logo" class="logo" />
</div>

<style>
	div {
		background-image: url('Softground-Hero.gif');
		background-size: cover;
	}

	.logo {
		animation: spin 5s linear infinite;
	}

	@keyframes spin {
		from {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		to {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
</style>
```

In this example, the logo will continuously spin. You can adjust the `5s` duration and other properties to fit your needs.

_**User (2025-01-19 22:53Z)**_

logo is svg so animate it drawing in

---

_**Assistant**_

To animate an SVG logo by drawing it in, you can use the `stroke-dasharray` and `stroke-dashoffset` properties in CSS. Here's an example of how to do it:

1. Ensure your SVG has a `stroke` property.
2. Apply the animation using CSS.

Here's how you can update your Svelte component:

```svelte
<div>
	<img src="SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg" alt="logo" class="logo" />
</div>

<style>
	div {
		background-image: url('Softground-Hero.gif');
		background-size: cover;
	}

	.logo {
		width: 200px; /* Adjust as needed */
		height: auto;
	}

	/* SVG animation */
	@keyframes draw {
		from {
			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 1000;
		}
		to {
			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 0;
		}
	}

	.logo path {
		stroke: #000; /* Adjust the stroke color as needed */
		stroke-width: 2; /* Adjust the stroke width as needed */
		fill: none;
		animation: draw 5s ease-in-out forwards;
	}
</style>
```

Make sure your SVG file (`SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg`) has `path` elements that can be animated. If your SVG doesn't have `path` elements, you may need to convert it to a format that does.

To animate the SVG paths by drawing them in, you need to ensure that the paths have a `stroke` property and that the CSS animation targets these paths correctly. Here's how you can update your Svelte component and SVG file:

1. Ensure the SVG paths have a `stroke` property.
2. Apply the animation using CSS.

### Updated SVG File (`SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg`)

Make sure your SVG paths have a `stroke` property. You can add this directly in the SVG file:


### Updated Svelte Component (`+page.svelte`)

Apply the animation using CSS:

```svelte
<div>
	<img src="SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg" alt="logo" class="logo" />
</div>

<style>
	div {
		background-image: url('Softground-Hero.gif');
		background-size: cover;
	}

	.logo {
		width: 200px; /* Adjust as needed */
		height: auto;
	}

	/* SVG animation */
	@keyframes draw {
		from {
			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 1000;
		}

			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 0;

		stroke: #000; /* Adjust the stroke color as needed */
		stroke-width: 2; /* Adjust the stroke width as needed */
		fill: none;
		animation: draw 5s ease-in-out forwards;
	}
</style>
```

This should animate the SVG paths by drawing them in. Adjust the `stroke-dasharray`, `stroke-dashoffset`, and animation duration as needed.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:05Z)**_

animate the fill not the stroke

---

_**Assistant**_

To animate the fill of the SVG paths instead of the stroke, you can use CSS animations to transition the `fill` property. Here's how you can update your Svelte component:

```svelte
<div>
	<svg
		id="Layer_1"
		data-name="Layer 1"
		xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
		viewBox="0 0 1001.01 533.34"
	>
		<defs>
			<style>
				.cls-1 {
					fill: #fff;
				}
			</style>
		</defs>
		<!-- Your SVG paths here -->
		<path class="cls-1" d="M517.62,118.06c-24.76-20.04-47.78-28.28-52.89-55.94-3.54-19.17.06-28.9.06-28.9,0,0-24.61,15.93-24.61,46.78,0,23.46,47.64,50.96,62.79,61.97,15.15,11.02,21.68,23.25,21.68,39.77,0,26-21.08,47.07-47.07,47.07-7.66,0-14.89-1.83-21.29-5.08,9.03,10.13,23.06,18.25,44.84,18.25,44.25,0,61.93-35.8,61.93-58.4,0-30.88-19.68-44.67-45.44-65.52Z"/>
		<!-- Add other paths as needed -->
	</svg>
</div>

<style>
	div {
		background-image: url('Softground-Hero.gif');
		background-size: cover;
	}

	@keyframes fillAnimation {
		from {
			fill: transparent;
		}
		to {
			fill: #fff; /* Adjust the final fill color as needed */
		}
	}

	#Layer_1 path {
		stroke: none; /* Ensure stroke is not visible */
		fill: transparent; /* Start with transparent fill */
		animation: fillAnimation 5s ease-in-out forwards;
	}
</style>
```

In this example, the `fillAnimation` keyframes transition the `fill` property from `transparent` to `#fff` over 5 seconds. Adjust the duration and colors as needed.

In this example, the `draw` keyframes animate the `stroke-dasharray` and `stroke-dashoffset` properties to create a drawing effect. Adjust the `stroke-dasharray`, `stroke-dashoffset`, and animation duration as needed.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:10Z)**_

use the MP4 stop when the video's done

---

_**Assistant**_

To use an MP4 video as the background and stop the animation when the video is done, you can use the `onended` event of the video element to remove the animation class from the SVG paths. Here's how you can update your Svelte component:

```svelte
<script>
	let videoEnded = false;

	function handleVideoEnd() {
		videoEnded = true;
	}
</script>

<div>
	<video autoplay muted onended={handleVideoEnd} class="background-video">
		<source src="Softground-Hero.mp4" type="video/mp4" />
		Your browser does not support the video tag.
	</video>
	<svg
		id="Layer_1"
		data-name="Layer 1"
		xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
		viewBox="0 0 1001.01 533.34"
		class:draw={!videoEnded}
	>
		<defs>
			<style>
				.cls-1 {
					fill: none;
					stroke: #fff; /* Adjust the stroke color as needed */
					stroke-width: 2; /* Adjust the stroke width as needed */
				}
			</style>
		</defs>
		<!-- Your SVG paths here -->
		<path class="cls-1" d="M517.62,118.06c-24.76-20.04-47.78-28.28-52.89-55.94-3.54-19.17.06-28.9.06-28.9,0,0-24.61,15.93-24.61,46.78,0,23.46,47.64,50.96,62.79,61.97,15.15,11.02,21.68,23.25,21.68,39.77,0,26-21.08,47.07-47.07,47.07-7.66,0-14.89-1.83-21.29-5.08,9.03,10.13,23.06,18.25,44.84,18.25,44.25,0,61.93-35.8,61.93-58.4,0-30.88-19.68-44.67-45.44-65.52Z"/>
		<!-- Add other paths as needed -->
	</svg>
</div>

<style>
	.background-video {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		object-fit: cover;
		z-index: -1;
	}

	div {
		position: relative;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
	}

	@keyframes draw {
		0% {
			fill: transparent;
			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 1000;
		}
		90% {
			fill: transparent;
			stroke-dasharray: 1000;
			stroke-dashoffset: 0;
		}
		100% {
			fill: #fff;
		}
	}

	.draw path {
		fill: transparent;
		stroke: #fff;
		animation: draw 2s ease-in forwards;
	}
</style>
```

In this example:
- The video is set to autoplay and muted.
- The `handleVideoEnd` function sets `videoEnded` to `true` when the video ends.
- The `draw` class is conditionally applied to the SVG based on the `videoEnded` state.
- The `draw` class triggers the animation, and it is removed when the video ends, stopping the animation.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:12Z)**_

Import svg as svelte component

---

_**Assistant**_

To import an SVG as a Svelte component, you can use the `svelte-inline-svg` package. This package allows you to import SVG files directly into your Svelte components. Here's how you can do it:

1. Install the `svelte-inline-svg` package.
2. Import the SVG file as a Svelte component.
3. Use the imported SVG component in your Svelte file.

### Step 1: Install `svelte-inline-svg`

Open your terminal and run the following command to install the package:

```sh
npm install svelte-inline-svg
```

### Step 2: Import the SVG File

In your Svelte component, import the SVG file using the `svelte-inline-svg` package:

```svelte
<script>
    import { onMount } from 'svelte';
    import SvgLogo from './SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svg';
    let videoEnded = false;

    function handleVideoEnd() {
        videoEnded = true;
    }
</script>

<div>
    <video autoplay muted onended={handleVideoEnd} class="background-video">
        <source src="Softground-Hero.mp4" type="video/mp4" />
        Your browser does not support the video tag.
    </video>
    <SvgLogo class:draw={!videoEnded} />
</div>

<style>
    .background-video {
        position: absolute;
        width: 100%;
        height: 100%;
        object-fit: cover;
        z-index: -1;
    }

    div {
        position: relative;
        width: 100%;
        height: 100%;
    }

    @keyframes draw {
        0% {
            fill: transparent;
            stroke-dasharray: 1000;
            stroke-dashoffset: 1000;
        }
        90% {
            fill: transparent;
            stroke-dasharray: 1000;
            stroke-dashoffset: 0;
        }
        100% {
            fill: #fff;
        }
    }

    .draw path {
        fill: transparent;
        stroke: #fff;
        animation: draw 5s ease-in-out forwards;
    }
</style>
```

### Step 3: Use the Imported SVG Component

In the example above, the `SvgLogo` component is used in place of the inline SVG code. The `class:draw={!videoEnded}` directive conditionally applies the `draw` class based on the `videoEnded` state.

This setup will animate the SVG paths by drawing them in when the video ends. Adjust the animation duration and properties as needed.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:25Z)**_

What is the root for import svelte

---

_**Assistant**_

In a Svelte project, the root for imports typically depends on your project's configuration, especially if you're using a bundler like Rollup or Vite. By default, you can use relative paths to import components and other files. However, you can also configure aliases for cleaner and more manageable imports.

### Default Import Paths

If you're using relative paths, you can import components like this:

```svelte
<script>
	import KOStackedLogo from './lib/SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svelte';
</script>
```

### Using Aliases

To use aliases, you need to configure your bundler. Here's how you can do it for Rollup and Vite:

#### Rollup

1. Install the `@rollup/plugin-alias` plugin:

   ```sh
   npm install @rollup/plugin-alias --save-dev
   ```

2. Update your `rollup.config.js`:

   ```js
   import alias from '@rollup/plugin-alias';
   import path from 'path';

   export default {
     // other configurations...
     plugins: [
       alias({
         entries: [
           { find: 'lib', replacement: path.resolve(__dirname, 'src/lib') }
         ]
       })
     ]
   };
   ```

#### Vite

1. Update your `vite.config.js`:

   ```js
   import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
   import { svelte } from '@sveltejs/vite-plugin-svelte';
   import path from 'path';

   export default defineConfig({
     resolve: {
       alias: {
         lib: path.resolve(__dirname, 'src/lib')
       }
     },
     plugins: [svelte()]
   });
   ```

### Using the Alias

Once you've configured the alias, you can import components like this:

```svelte
<script>
	import KOStackedLogo from 'lib/SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svelte';
</script>
```

This makes your imports cleaner and easier to manage, especially in larger projects.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:36Z)**_

fade filter in after 15 seconds

---

_**Assistant**_

To fade in the `.hero-filter` after 15 seconds, you can use CSS animations. Here's how you can update your Svelte component to achieve this effect:

```svelte
<script>
	import KOStackedLogo from '../lib/SG_Logo+Tagline_Stacked_KO.svelte';
	let videoEnded = false;

	function handleVideoEnd() {
		videoEnded = true;
	}
</script>

<div class="hero">
	<KOStackedLogo />
	<div class="hero-filter"></div>
	<video autoplay muted onended={handleVideoEnd} class="background-video">
		<source src="SoftgroundHero.mp4" type="video/mp4" />
		Your browser does not support the video tag.
	</video>
</div>

<style>
	.background-video {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		object-fit: cover;
		z-index: -1;
		top: 0;
	}

	.hero {
		position: relative;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
	}

	.hero-filter {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		background-color: rgba(20, 62, 5, 0.5);
		top: 0;
		opacity: 0;
		animation: fadeIn 2s ease-in forwards;
		animation-delay: 15s;
	}

	@keyframes fadeIn {
		to {
			opacity: 1;
		}
	}
</style>
```

In this example:
- The `.hero-filter` element starts with `opacity: 0`.
- The `fadeIn` animation changes the opacity to `1` over 2 seconds.
- The `animation-delay: 15s` property delays the start of the animation by 15 seconds.

_**User (2025-01-19 23:51Z)**_

global variable

---

_**Assistant**_

To define and use global CSS variables, you can declare them in the `:root` selector in your `global.css` file. Here's how you can do it:

### Step 1: Define Global CSS Variables

Add the CSS variables to the `:root` selector in your `global.css` file:

```css
:root {
    --primary-color: red;
    --secondary-color: blue;
    --background-color: #f0f0f0;
    --font-size: 16px;
}

body {
    background: var(--background-color);
    font-size: var(--font-size);
}
```

### Step 2: Use the CSS Variables

You can now use these variables throughout your CSS files. For example, in your `+page.svelte` file:

```svelte
<style>
	.background-video {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		object-fit: cover;
		z-index: -1;
		top: 0;
	}

	.hero {
		position: relative;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
	}

	.hero-filter {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		background-color: rgba(20, 62, 5, 0.5);
		top: 0;
		opacity: 0;
		animation: fadeIn 2s ease-in forwards;
		animation-delay: 15s;
	}

	@keyframes fadeIn {
		to {
			opacity: 1;
		}
	}

	h1 {
		color: var(--primary-color);
	}

	h2 {
		color: var(--secondary-color);
	}
</style>
```

In this example:
- The `--primary-color`, `--secondary-color`, `--background-color`, and `--font-size` variables are defined in `global.css`.
- These variables are then used in the `+page.svelte` file to style the `h1` and `h2` elements.

---

_**User (2025-01-19 23:57Z)**_

load custom fonts

---

_**Assistant**_

To load custom fonts in your `global.css` file, you can use the `@font-face` rule. Here's how you can do it:

### Step 1: Add the `@font-face` Rule

Add the `@font-face` rule to your `global.css` file to define the custom fonts:

```css
@font-face {
    font-family: 'CustomFont';
    src: url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Regular.woff2') format('woff2'),
         url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Regular.woff') format('woff');
    font-weight: normal;
    font-style: normal;
}

@font-face {
    font-family: 'CustomFont';
    src: url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Bold.woff2') format('woff2'),
         url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Bold.woff') format('woff');
    font-weight: bold;
    font-style: normal;
}

body {
    background: var(--primary-background);
    font-family: 'CustomFont', sans-serif;
}

:root {
    --primary-background: #143E05;
    --primary-shadow: #006A33;
    --primary: #009638;
    --highlight: #00B737;
}
```

### Step 2: Use the Custom Fonts

You can now use the custom fonts throughout your CSS files. For example, in your `+page.svelte` file:

```svelte
<style>
	.background-video {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		object-fit: cover;
		z-index: -1;
		top: 0;
	}

	.hero {
		position: relative;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
	}

	.hero-filter {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		background-color: rgba(20, 62, 5, 0.5);
		top: 0;
		opacity: 0;
		animation: fadeIn 2s ease-in forwards;
		animation-delay: 15s;
	}

	@keyframes fadeIn {
		to {
			opacity: 1;
		}
	}

	h1, h2 {
		font-family: 'CustomFont', sans-serif;
	}
</style>
```

In this example:
- The `@font-face` rule is used to define the custom font `CustomFont`.
- The `font-family` property is set to use `CustomFont` for the `body` element and the `h1` and `h2` elements. Adjust the paths to your font files as needed.

---

_**User (2025-01-19 23:57Z)**_

i only have ttf files

---

_**Assistant**_

If you only have TTF (TrueType Font) files, you can still use them with the `@font-face` rule in your `global.css` file. Here's how you can do it:

### Step 1: Add the `@font-face` Rule

Add the `@font-face` rule to your `global.css` file to define the custom fonts using TTF files:

```css
@font-face {
    font-family: 'CustomFont';
    src: url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Regular.ttf') format('truetype');
    font-weight: normal;
    font-style: normal;
}

@font-face {
    font-family: 'CustomFont';
    src: url('/path/to/fonts/CustomFont-Bold.ttf') format('truetype');
    font-weight: bold;
    font-style: normal;
}

body {
    background: var(--primary-background);
    font-family: 'CustomFont', sans-serif;
}

:root {
    --primary-background: #143E05;
    --primary-shadow: #006A33;
    --primary: #009638;
    --highlight: #00B737;
}
```

### Step 2: Use the Custom Fonts

You can now use the custom fonts throughout your CSS files. For example, in your `+page.svelte` file:

```svelte
<style>
	.background-video {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		object-fit: cover;
		z-index: -1;
		top: 0;
	}

	.hero {
		position: relative;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
	}

	.hero-filter {
		position: absolute;
		width: 100%;
		height: 100%;
		background-color: rgba(20, 62, 5, 0.5);
		top: 0;
		opacity: 0;
		animation: fadeIn 2s ease-in forwards;
		animation-delay: 15s;
	}

	@keyframes fadeIn {
		to {
			opacity: 1;
		}
	}

	h1, h2 {
		font-family: 'CustomFont', sans-serif;
	}
</style>
```

In this example:
- The `@font-face` rule is used to define the custom font `CustomFont` using TTF files.
- The `font-family` property is set to use `CustomFont` for the `body` element and the `h1` and `h2` elements. Adjust the paths to your font files as needed.
